Bradfute, Charles H. Calisher, Boris Klempa, Jonas Klingström, Jens H. Kuhn, Lies Laenen, Nicole D. Tischler and Piet Maes The citation for this ICTV Report chapter is the summary published as: Bradfute, S. B., Calisher, C. H., Klempa, B., Klingström, J., Kuhn, J. H., Laenen, L., Tischler, N. D., & Maes, P. (2024). ICTV Virus Taxonomy Profile: Hantaviridae 2024, Journal of General Virology 105, 001975 Corresponding author: Piet Maes ( [email protected] ) Edited by: Holly Hughes and Evelien Adriaenssens Posted: March 2024, updated June 2024 Summary Hantaviridae is a family for negative-sense RNA viruses with genomes of about 10.5–14.6 kb (Table 1 Hantaviridae ). The family includes eight genera ( Actinovirus and Percilovirus (subfamily Actantavirinae ), Agnathovirus (subfamily Agantavirinae ), Loanvirus , Mobatvirus , Orthohantavirus and Thottimvirus (subfamily Mammantavirinae ), and Reptillovirus (subfamily Repantavirinae ). These viruses are maintained in and/or transmitted by small mammals, but also by fish and reptiles. Several orthohantaviruses can infect humans, causing mild, severe, and sometimes-fatal diseases. Hantavirids produce enveloped virions containing three single-stranded RNA segments with open reading frames (ORFs) that encode a nucleoprotein (N), a glycoprotein precursor (GPC), and large (L) protein containing an RNA-directed RNA polymerase (RdRP) domain. Table 1 Hantaviridae. Characteristics of members of the family Hantaviridae. Characteristic Description* Example Hantaan virus (S segment: M14626; M segment: M14627; L segment: X55901), species Orthohantavirus hantanense Virion Enveloped, pleomorphic virions 80–160 nm in diameter with heterodimeric surface spikes Genome Three single-stranded RNA molecules (segments): small (S; 1.0–3.0 kb), medium (M; 3.4–4.8 kb), and large (L; 5.3–6.8 kb) Replication Ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes containing anti-genomic RNA serve as coding templates for synthesis of genomic RNA Translation Proteins are produced from capped and non-polyadenylated mRNAs. The 5′-cap structure is obtained via cap-snatching from cellular mRNAs Host range Fish (actinoviruses, agnathoviruses, perciloviruses), mammals (orthohantaviruses), and reptiles (reptilloviruses) Taxonomy Realm Riboviria , kingdom Orthornavirae , phylum Negarnaviricota , class Bunyaviricetes , order Elliovirales ; the family includes four subfamilies, eight genera, and 55 species * mostly based on experiments with mammalian orthohantaviruses Viruses assigned to each of the seven genera form genus-specifc monophyletic clades based on phylogenetic analysis of concatenated L protein/RdRP, glycoprotein and N sequences. Viruses from all seven genera share one or more of the following characteristics: (i) enveloped pleomorphic virions; (ii) tri-segmented, negative-sense RNA genome without polyadenylated tracts at the 3′-end; (iii) genomic sequence complementarity at the 5′- and 3′-ends; and (iv) capped but not polyadenylated virus mRNAs ( Maes et al., 2004 ). Piscine Hosts Genus Actinovirus . Viruses in this genus infect actinopterygian (percid, ogcocephalid, and triacanthodid, and possibly goblid and melanotaeniid) fish. Genus Agnathovirus . Viruses in this genus infect agnathan (myxinid) fish. Genus Percilovirus . Viruses in this genus infect actinopterygian ( gobiid, lophiid ) fish. Mammalian Hosts Genus Loanvirus. Viruses in this genus infect rhinolophid, verspertillionid, and possibly nycterid bats and possibly murid rodents. Genus Mobatvirus. Viruses in this genus infect emballonurid, hipposiderid, pteropodid and possibly molossid, and vespertillionid bats and soricid and talpid eulipotyphla. Genus Orthohantavirus. Viruses assigned to this genus infect soricid and talpid eulipotyphla and muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents. Genus Thottimvirus. Viruses in this genus infect soricid and possibly talpid eulipotyphla. Reptilian Hosts Genus Reptillovirus . Viruses in this genus infect gekkonid and possibly scincid reptiles. Virion Morphology Only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Physicochemical and physical properties Only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Nucleic acid Hantavirids have tri-segmented, negative-sense RNA genomes (S, M, and L segments). The viral mRNAs are not polyadenylated and contain a 5′-methylated cap and several non-templated nucleotides at the 5′-end that are derived from host cell mRNAs via cap snatching ( Schmaljohn et al., 1985 , Jonsson and Schmaljohn 2001 , Vaheri et al., 2013 ). Further information is only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Proteins Hantavirids typically express three structural proteins. The most abundant structural protein in a hantavirion is N, which encapsidates the hantaviral genomic segments. The least abundant protein is L, which mediates viral genome replication and transcription. Two glycoprotein subunits, G N and G C , are produced from GPC and mediate entry of hantavirions ( Schmaljohn et al., 1985 , Spiropoulou 2001 , Vaheri et al., 2013 ). Specifics are only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Lipids Not reported but are likely derived from the Golgi apparatus and/or the host cell plasma membrane and therefore likely composed of phospholipids, glycolipids, fatty acids, and sterols. Carbohydrates Only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Genome organization and replication S RNA encodes N and in some viruses NSs ( Jääskeläinen et al., 2007 , Vera-Otarola et al., 2012 ), M RNA encodes GPC, and the L RNA encodes L with its RdRP, helicase, and endonuclease domains ( Jonsson and Schmaljohn 2001 ) (Figure 1 Hantaviridae ). Specifics are only known for members of the genus Orthohantavirus (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Figure 1 Hantaviridae . Genome organization of representative hantaviruses. The 5′- and 3′- ends of all segments are complementary at their termini, likely promoting the formation of panhandle RNP complexes within the virion . GPC , glycoprotein precursor gene; L , large (protein) gene; N , nucleoprotein gene. Biology Hantavirids have been found in actinopterygian (gobiid, lophiid, percid, ogcocephalid, and triacanthodid, and possibly goblid and melanotaeniid) and agnathan (myxinid) fish; in emballonurid, hipposiderid, pteropodid, rhinolophid, verspertillionid, and possibly molossid and nycterid bats; in soricid and talpid eulipotyphla; in muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents; in gekkonid and possibly scincid reptiles; and potentially in coleopteran and plectopteran insects (see genus pages for details). Only orthohantaviruses are known to cause diseases (see Orthohantavirus genus page). Antigenicity Systematic antigenicity studies have only been reported for orthohantaviruses (see Orthohantavirus genus page). England thailandense : after Thailand thottapalayamense : after Thottapalayam , India Thottimvirus : after Thott opalayam virus tigrayense : after ትግራይ ( Tigray Region), Ethiopia triacanthodis : after the fish genus Triacanthod es tulaense : after Тула ( Tula ), Russia xuansonense : after Xuân Sơn , Vietnam wufangense : after after Wùfēng (吾锋), Fújiàn Province (福建省), China Subfamily, genus and species demarcation criteria The availability of at least coding-complete sequences of all three genome segments may be sufficient for hantavirid classification in the absence of a cultured isolate. Demarcation of genera is based upon DivErsity pArtitioning by hieRarchical Clustering (DEmARC) analysis) using concatenated deduced S, M, and L segment expression product sequences ( Hantaviridae: current classification and future perspectives, Viruses, 11, 9, e0788">Laenen et al., 2019 ). DEmARC analysis gave a frequency distribution of Pairwise Evolutionary Distance (PED) values of which the threshold of 0.1 gave an optimal clustering cost of zero and is used as the hantavirid species demarcation criterium. Genera are demarked by a PED-value threshold of 0.95 and subfamilies are demarcated based on their distinct clustering in a Bayesian maximum clade credibility tree and a PED-value threshold of 3.5. Relationships within the family Phylogenetic relationships across the family have been estimated using Bayesian maximum clade credibility trees generated from complete N, GPC, and L amino-acid sequences; similar topologies are obtained with concatenated N and GPC sequences (Figure 2 Hantaviridae ). Figure 2 Hantaviridae . Maximum likelihood phylogenetic tree inferred using IQ-TREE. The tree was constructed based on 53 concatenated S and M segment (amino acid) sequences using the LG+R9 model of sequence evolution. Branch lengths represent the estimated number of substitutions per site. Bootstrap support values (>70%) are indicated at nodes, reflecting the degree of confidence in the branching pattern. Relationships with other taxa Hantavirids are closely related to bunyaviral crulivirids, fimovirids, peribunyavirids, phasmavirids, tulasvirids, and tospovirids ( Wolf et al., 2018 , Herath et al., 2020 ). Related, unclassified viruses Virus name Accession number Virus abbreviation Reference coleopteran hanta-related virus OKIAV221 L: MT153497 ( Käfer et al., 2019 ) plecopteran hanta-related virus OKIAV215 L: MT153434 ( Käfer et al., 2019 ) Virus names and virus abbreviations are not official ICTV designations. Additional unclassified hantavirids that are probable members of established genera are listed under individual genus descriptions. Using the Master Species List Using the Virus Metadata Resource Using the Find the Species tool Using the ICTV Report Webinar Webinar on Virus Taxonomy Webinar on Finding Information GitHub Repository Zenodo Repository Email ICTV Help ‏‏‎ ‎ ICTV Report Book: Hantaviridae Family: Hantaviridae Subfamily: Actantavirinae Genus: Actinovirus Genus: Percilovirus Subfamily: Agantavirinae Genus: Agnathovirus Subfamily: Mammantavirinae Genus: Loanvirus Genus: Mobatvirus Genus: Orthohantavirus Genus: Thottimvirus Subfamily: Repantavirinae Genus: Reptillovirus Authors: Hantaviridae Citation: Hantaviridae References: Hantaviridae Resources: Hantaviridae Species List: Hantaviridae Subfamily: Mammantavirinae Genus: Orthohantavirus Distinguishing features Sixty different viruses are classified in the genus Orthohantavius. These viruses typically infect specific soricid and talpid eulipotyphla and muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents. Orthohantaviruses are the only hantavirids known to cause disease in humans ( Schmaljohn and Hjelle 1997 , Jonsson et al., 2010 , Kuhn and Schmaljohn 2023 , Vial et al., 2023 ). Virion Morphology Orthohantavirions are pleomorphic in shape, mostly spherical of 80–160 nm in diameter ( Huiskonen et al., 2010 ), and surrounded by a membrane envelope that is decorated with glycoprotein (GP) spikes composed of G N and G C subunits (Figure 1 Orthohantavirus ). The virion envelope is 5 nm thick and is decorated with square shaped spikes, consisting of tetrameric G N -G C heterodimers, which protrude 10 nM from the membrane forming a surface lattice of local symmetry interrupted by empty membrane spots ( Hung et al., 1983 , Huiskonen et al., 2010 , Battisti et al., 2011 , Parvate et al., 2019 , Serris et al., 2020 , Guardado-Calvo and Rey 2021 ). Isolated ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes are composed of individual segment genomic RNA encapsidated by nucleoprotein (N) ( Tao et al., 1987 , Huiskonen et al., 2010 , Battisti et al., 2011 , Hepojoki et al., 2012 , Guo et al., 2016 , Arragain et al., 2019 ). Virions assemble and bud from the Golgi complex or cell surface membranes, depending on the virus ( Goldsmith et al., 1995 , Parvate et al., 2019 ). Figure 1 Orthohantavirus. A, B) Electron micrographs of Tula virus particles adapted from ( Huiskonen et al., 2010 ). C) Schematic illustration of an orthohantavirus particle. Shown is a spherical and enveloped (grey) particle with glycoprotein spikes (G N yellow, G C blue) inserted in a bilaminar lipid envelope. The S (small), M (medium), and L (large) RNP (ribonucleoprotein) complexes inside the particle consist of N (nucleoprotein, green) and L (large protein, pink). ViralZone image reproduced courtesy of SwissBioPics. Physicochemical and physical properties Virion buoyant densities in sucrose and CsCl are 1.15–1.18 ( White et al., 1982 ) and 1.20–1.21 g cm −3 , respectively ( Schmaljohn et al., 1983 ). Virions are sensitive to chemical inactivation with, for instance, methanol, paraformaldehyde, acetone/methanol, N -ethylmaleimide or detergents, as well as physical methods such as heat, gamma irradiation or UV irradiation ( Kraus et al., 2005 , Strandin et al., 2011 , Elveborg et al., 2022 ). Nucleic acid Orthohantavirus genomes are linear tri-segmented RNA molecules of negative polarity. The genomes are about 11.1–12.3 kb in length (small [S] segment: 1.2–2.1 kb; medium [M] segment: 3.4–3.8 kb; large [L] segment: 6.5–6.6 kb). Proteins Orthohantaviruses typically express three structural proteins (Table 1 Orthohantavirus ). The most abundant structural protein in an orthohantavirion is N, which encapsidates the orthohantaviral genomic segments. The least abundant protein is L, which mediates viral genome replication and transcription. Two glycoprotein subunits, G N and G C , mediate virion entry. G N and G C and non-structural proteins are encoded as a precursor polyprotein. Table 1 Orthohantavirus . Location and function of orthohantavirus structural proteins. Protein Location, mass, and function Reference Nucleoprotein (N) Structural virion protein (about 48 kD). Oligomerizes and encapsidates orthohantaviral genomic segments and hence component of RNPs. Binds to L and G N ( Mir et al., 2010 , Reuter and Krüger 2018 , Arragain et al., 2019 ) Nonstructural protein (NSs) Non-structural protein encoded by some orhtohantaviruses. Has type I interferon inhibitory activity. May occur as multiple isoforms. ( Vera-Otarola et al., 2020 , Binder et al., 2021 ) Glycoprotein (GP) Structural virion protein consisting of two subunits (G N : about 70-75 kD, G C : about 50-55 kD). Produced via proteolytic cleavage from the genome-encoded precursor GPC. Projects from virion membranes as tetrameric GP spikes composed of G N and G C heterodimers. GP mediates cell- receptor binding; as a class II fusion machine it induces virion-cell membrane fusion and, thereby cell entry. G N binds to N and RNA ( Spiropoulou et al., 2003 , Tischler et al., 2005 , Cifuentes-Muñoz et al., 2014 , Li et al., 2016 , Willensky et al., 2016 , Rissanen et al., 2017 , Zhu et al., 2018 , Bignon et al., 2019 , Sperber et al., 2019 ) Large protein (L) Structural virion protein (246 kD) with RdRP, helicase, and endoribonuclease domains. Component of the RNP inside virions. Binds to N and RNA. Oligomerizes and mediates transcription and replication of viral RNA segments. Mediates cap-snatching for viral mRNA capping. ( Kukkonen et al., 2005 , Cheng et al., 2014 , Rothenberger et al., 2016 , Jeeva et al., 2019 , Durieux Trouilleton et al., 2023 ) Genome organization and replication The orthohantavirus genome consists of three negative-sense RNA molecules, termed S (small), M (medium), and L (large) (Figure 2 Orthohantavirus ). Figure 2 Orthohantavirus. Schematic representation of orthohantavirus genome organization. Orthohantavirus genomic segments assume circular forms via non-covalent binding of complementary and highly conserved 3′- and 5′-terminal sequences. Genomic segment reassortment mostly occurs among viruses belonging to the same species but interspecies reassortment likely occurs occasionally ( Klempa 2018 , Petazzi et al., 2021 ). Orthohantavirus infection starts with virion attachment, mediated by G N and G C , to cell-surface attachment factors and entry via the endosomal route ( Shtanko et al., 2014 ) (Figure 3 Orthohantavirus ). Figure 3 Orthohantavirus . Lifecycle of orthohantaviruses. (1) Virion attachment; (2) virion uptake; (3) virion-cell membrane fusion; (4) transcription; (5) translation; (6) replication; (7) virion assembly; and (8) virion egress. After engaging a cellular receptor, protocadherin 1 in the case of American orthohantaviruses, virion-membrane fusion results in early or late endosomal release, depending on the virus, of the virion RNP complex into the cytoplasm in a pH-dependent manner ( Jangra et al., 2018 , Mittler et al., 2019 , Torriani et al., 2019 , Dieterle et al., 2021 ). During primary transcription L generates uncapped antigenomic RNA molecules that are then capped using host cell-derived capped primers (cap snatching). L and S segment-transcribed mRNAs are translated by free ribosomes ( Garcin et al., 1995 , Jeeva et al., 2019 , Meier et al., 2021 ). M segment-transcribed mRNA is translated by membrane-bound ribosomes, with the expressed GPC which is likely co-translationally cleaved by cellular proteases to yield G N and G C . The synthesis of the antigenome by L protein serves as a template for genomic RNA replication. Secondary transcription amplifies the synthesis of mRNAs and genome replication. During morphogenesis, G N and G C accumulate in the Golgi apparatus, modified host membranes are acquired, and virions bud into the Golgi. Biology Genus Orthohantavirus includes 38 species for 60 distinct viruses. These viruses and their unclassified, potential relatives, subclinically, and often chronically, infect specific soricid and talpid eulipotyphla, and muroid and possibly dipodoid rodents ( Jonsson et al., 2010 , Wu et al., 2018 , Chen et al., 2023 , Kuhn and Schmaljohn 2023 ). Orthohantaviruses are overall distributed worldwide, but the individual geographic distribution is dependent on host range. At least some orthohantaviruses may not be as restricted to a particular host as originally thought, but may be carried by animals of multiple hosts ( Goodfellow et al., 2021 , Quizon et al., 2022 , Gu et al., 2023 ). Orthohantaviruses are the only hantavirids known to cause disease (all in humans) and all disease-causing viruses are rodent-borne. Human infection may occur after inhalation of aerosolized excreta or secreta of infected rodents or direct rodent contact. The two WHO-recognized orthohantavirus-caused diseases are hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS), caused by Asian and European viruses (in particular Dobrava virus [DOBV], Hantaan virus [HTNV], Puumala virus [PUUV] and Seoul virus [SEOV]), and hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS), a frequently lethal disease caused by viruses from the Americas (in particular Andes virus [ANDV] and Sin Nombre virus [SNV]) ( Vial et al., 2023 ). The hamster model of wild-type ANDV infection is lethal and largely mimics human HPS ( Hooper et al., 2001 ). After SNV infection in nonhuman primates, some animals develop an HPS-like disease, but cell passage of SNV can result in mutations that render the virus non-pathogenic in this model ( Safronetz et al., 2014 ). Many rodent models result in a chronic, asymptomatic infection, and these have been used in early-stage therapeutic studies ( Strandin et al., 2020 ). However, recent advances have been made in development of an HFRS mouse model using HTNV variants ( Wei et al., 2022 ). Orthohantavirus animal models are reviewed in ( Golden et al., 2015 ). Antigenicity There is considerable serum cross-reactivity between the nucleocapsids of hantavirids, but neutralizing antibodies targeting Gn/Gc are more specific. SNV was first identified as a hantavirid based on cross-reactivity of patient sera with other orthohantavirus antigens ( 1993 ). Similarly, rodents that carried SNV were identified in serosurveys using non-SNV orthohantavirus antigens, since SNV had not been isolated at the time ( Childs et al., 1994 ). Species demarcation criteria Demarcation of species is based upon DivErsity pArtitioning by hieRarchical Clustering (DEmARC) analysis) using concatenated deduced S, M, and L segment expression product sequences ( Laenen et al., 2019 ). Phylogenetic relationships across the genus have been estimated using maximum likelihood trees generated from complete N, GPC and L amino-acid sequences, and for N and GPC amino-acid sequences (Figure 2 Hantaviridae ). Hantaviridae ~ ViralZone Skip to main content ViralZone Hantaviridae (taxid:1980413) VIRION Enveloped, spherical. Diameter from 80 to 120nm. GENOME Segmented Negative-stranded RNA linear genome, L segment is between 6.8 and 12 kb, M segment between 3.2 and 4.9 kb and S segment between 1 and 3 kb. Encodes for four to six proteins. Some hantaviruses also encode a NSs protein on their S segment. GENE EXPRESSION The viral RNA dependent RNA polymerase (L) binds to a promoter on each encapsidated segment, and transcribes the mRNA. These are capped by L protein during synthesis using cap snatching . Transcription is terminated by a strong hairpin sequence at the end of each gene. Puumala virus expressed an additional NSs by leaky scanning in the NS gene. ENZYMES RNA-dependent RNA polymerase L Cap Snatching endonuclease L REPLICATION CYTOPLASMIC Virus attaches to host receptors though Gn-Gc glycoprotein dimer, and is endocytosed into vesicles in the host cell. Fusion of virus membrane with the vesicle membrane; ribonucleocapsid segments are released in the cytoplasm. Transcription , viral mRNAs are capped in the cytoplasm. Replication presumably starts when enough nucleoprotein is present to encapsidate neo-synthetized antigenomes and genomes. The ribonucleocapsids buds at Golgi apparatus, releasing the virion by exocytosis. Orthohantavirus ~ ViralZone Skip to main content ViralZone Orthohantavirus (taxid:1980442) Orthohantavirus is a genus of ssRNA- viruses in the Hantaviridae family that infect Rodents and rarely humans. In humans, these viruses can cause hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome, or Hantavirus pulmonary syndromes. VIRION Enveloped, spherical. Diameter from 80 to 120nm. GENOME Segmented Negative-stranded RNA linear genome, L segment is between 6.8 and 12 kb, M segment between 3.2 and 4.9 kb and S segment between 1 and 3 kb. Encodes for four to six proteins. Some hantaviruses also encode a NSs protein on their S segment. GENE EXPRESSION The viral RNA dependent RNA polymerase (L) binds to a promoter on each encapsidated segment, and transcribes the mRNA. These are capped by L protein during synthesis using cap snatching . Transcription is terminated by a strong hairpin sequence at the end of each gene. mRNAs are capped by L protein during synthesis. Puumala virus expressed an additional NSs by leaky scanning in the NS gene. ENZYMES RNA-dependent RNA polymerase L Cap Snatching endonuclease L REPLICATION CYTOPLASMIC Virus attaches to host receptors though Gn-Gc glycoprotein dimer, and is endocytosed into vesicles in the host cell. Fusion of virus membrane with the vesicle membrane; ribonucleocapsid segments are released in the cytoplasm. Transcription , viral mRNAs are capped in the cytoplasm. Replication presumably starts when enough nucleoprotein is present to encapsidate neo-synthetized antigenomes and genomes. The ribonucleocapsids buds at Golgi apparatus, releasing the virion by exocytosis. The hantaviruses (Hantaviridae) ~ ViralZone Skip to main content ViralZone Back to Andes virus resource There are approximately 92 known viruses in the family Hantaviridae, classified into 52 species. Each species is typically adapted to a specific reservoir host, most commonly rodents, but also bats and moles. With the notable exception of Andes virus, hantaviruses are generally not transmitted from person to person. Based on their geographic distribution, evolutionary relationships, and the diseases they cause in humans, hantaviruses can be broadly divided into three major groups: New World hantaviruses (the Americas) These viruses cause Hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HPS), a severe respiratory disease. Important examples include Sin Nombre virus and Andes virus. Annual incidence are about 100 cases per year (Andes virus) about 25 cases per year (Sin Nombre virus) Old World hantaviruses (Asia) These viruses are responsible for Hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS), which is characterized by fever, hemorrhage, and acute kidney injury. Major examples include Hantaan virus and Seoul virus. Annual incidence: more than 10,000 cases worldwide Old World hantaviruses (Europe) European hantaviruses typically cause a milder form of HFRS known as Nephropathia epidemica (NE). The main causative agents are Puumala virus and Dobrava-Belgrade virus. Annual incidence: more than 1,000 cases Together, these viruses represent an important and diverse group of zoonotic pathogens that can cause severe disease following accidental transmission from their natural animal hosts to humans.

papers.title: Hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome in children: a systematic scoping review of presentation, critical care management, and outcomes.
papers.abstract: BACKGROUND: Pediatric hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome/hantavirus pulmonary syndrome (HCPS/HPS) is rare but can progress rapidly from a nonspecific febrile prodrome to respiratory failure, shock, and multiorgan dysfunction. Pediatric-specific evidence is limited, and a comprehensive map of the clinical literature is lacking. METHODS: We conducted a systematic scoping review per Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidance (protocol registered at DOI:10.17605/OSF.IO/MZDN4). PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Scopus, Web of Science, and Cochrane CENTRAL were searched from inception to May 2026. Eligible reports described children or adolescents (age ≤18 years) with HCPS/HPS and extractable pediatric clinical data. Two reviewers independently screened records, assessed overlap, extracted data, and appraised reporting quality using Joanna Briggs Institute tools. RESULTS: Of 2208 database records, 20 reports were included in the core pediatric synthesis after removal of 976 duplicates and screening. Reports were concentrated in the Americas. Recurrent features included fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, myalgia, thrombocytopenia, hemoconcentration, pulmonary edema, respiratory failure, and shock. Severe cases required mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation, and in some cases renal replacement therapy. Larger pediatric case series and surveillance-level reports reported mortality of approximately 33% to 37%. Evidence was predominantly case-based with variable reporting completeness. CONCLUSIONS: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but potentially rapidly fatal. Early suspicion in endemic regions or after rodent exposure, with prompt supportive critical care and timely consideration of advanced cardiopulmonary support, is central to management.

analysis.background: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but can progress rapidly to severe disease, prompting a need to synthesize limited pediatric data (A1).
analysis.main_directions: The review maps pediatric clinical presentation and critical‑care interventions such as mechanical ventilation, vasoactive support, ECMO and renal replacement therapy (A9).
analysis.current_state: Mortality in reported pediatric series is about 33‑37%, but the evidence is mainly case‑based with variable reporting completeness (A11, A12).
analysis.gaps: Pediatric‑specific evidence is scarce and geographically limited, and reporting completeness is inconsistent, hindering robust conclusions (A2, A12).
analysis.future_research: Prospective multicenter surveillance, standardized case reporting in endemic areas, and studies on optimal timing of advanced cardiopulmonary support are needed (A2, A12).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Pediatric HCPS/HPS is rare but can rapidly become fatal, necessitating synthesis of limited data. Main directions: The review catalogs pediatric presentation, critical‑care measures (ventilation, vasoactive drugs, ECMO, renal replacement) and outcomes. Current state: Reported mortality is ~33‑37%, yet evidence is largely case‑based with variable completeness. Gaps: Pediatric‑specific data are scarce, geographically concentrated, and inconsistently reported. Future research: Prospective multicenter surveillance, standardized reporting, and trials on optimal timing of advanced cardiopulmonary support are required.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒心肺综合征 在儿童中的研究:关于表现、危重症管理与结局的系统性范围综述

analysis_zh.background: 儿科 HCPS/HPS 很少见,但可以迅速进展为严重疾病,因此需要综合有限的儿科数据 (A1)。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 该综述描绘了儿科临床表现和重症监护干预措施,如机械通气、血管活性支持、ECMO 和肾脏替代治疗 (A9)。
analysis_zh.current_state: 报告的儿科系列中的死亡率约为 33-37%,但证据主要是基于病例的,报告完整性各不相同 (A11、A12)。
analysis_zh.gaps: 儿科特定证据稀缺且地域有限,报告完整性不一致,阻碍了强有力的结论(A2,A12)。
analysis_zh.future_research: 需要进行前瞻性多中心监测、流行地区标准化病例报告以及对高级心肺支持最佳时机的研究(A2、A12)。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：儿科 HCPS/HPS 很少见,但可以迅速进展为严重疾病,因此需要综合有限的儿科数据 (A1)。 主要方向：该综述描绘了儿科临床表现和重症监护干预措施,如机械通气、血管活性支持、ECMO 和肾脏替代治疗 (A9)。 研究现状：报告的儿科系列中的死亡率约为 33-37%,但证据主要是基于病例的,报告完整性各不相同 (A11、A12)。 不足：儿科特定证据稀缺且地域有限,报告完整性不一致,阻碍了强有力的结论(A2,A12)。 后续研究：需要进行前瞻性多中心监测、流行地区标准化病例报告以及对高级心肺支持最佳时机的研究(A2、A12)。

papers.title: Can hantaviruses cause pandemics?
papers.abstract: Abstract The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns regarding the pandemic potential of hantaviruses, particularly because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proved human-to-human transmission. These concerns and fears are justified by the profound negative impact of the recent COVID-19 pandemic. Hantaviruses are segmented negative-sense RNA viruses maintained mainly in rodents and transmitted to man primarily through inhalation of contaminated aerosols. Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome predominates in Europe and Asia, whereas hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome occurs in North and South Americas and carries high mortality. Although most hantaviruses have no human-to-human transmissibility, ANDV outbreaks demonstrate that transmission between humans is possible under close-contact conditions. Comparison with previous pandemics and WHO priority pathogens highlights several pandemic-associated features shared by hantaviruses, including zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences. In this review, we will try to find the answer for the question “Can hantaviruses cause the next pandemic?”.
papers.journal: The Egyptian Journal of Internal Medicine

papers.full_text: Abstract The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns regarding the pandemic potential of hantaviruses, particularly because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proved human-to-human transmission. These concerns and fears are justified by the profound negative impact of the recent COVID-19 pandemic. Hantaviruses are segmented negative-sense RNA viruses maintained mainly in rodents and transmitted to man primarily through inhalation of contaminated aerosols. Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome predominates in Europe and Asia, whereas hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome occurs in North and South Americas and carries high mortality. Although most hantaviruses have no human-to-human transmissibility, ANDV outbreaks demonstrate that transmission between humans is possible under close-contact conditions. Comparison with previous pandemics and WHO priority pathogens highlights several pandemic-associated features shared by hantaviruses, including zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences. In this review, we will try to find the answer for the question “Can hantaviruses cause the next pandemic?”. Similar content being viewed by others Introduction On May 2, 2026, a deadly outbreak of hantavirus infection on the cruise ship MV Hondius in the Atlantic Ocean was reported. At that time, according to the ship operator, 147 passengers and crew members remained onboard, while 34 individuals had previously disembarked. By 13 May 2026, the ongoing outbreak had resulted in a total of 11 confirmed cases and three deaths, corresponding to a case fatality rate of approximately 27%.The early reported cases were laboratory-confirmed as Andes virus (ANDV) infections [1]. ANDV is a member of the family Hantaviridae .Other members of the family are Hantaan virus (HTNV), Seoul virus (SEOV), Dobrava-Belgrade virus (DOBV), Puumala virus (PUUV) and Sin Nombre virus (SNV) and all are highly pathogenic to humans [2]. News and reports about this outbreak drew attention of the public as well as the scientific community and an important question emerged, Can this outbreak on the cruise lead to a pandemic? In this article, we will review the current knowledge on hantaviruses, and lessons from the past pandemics, trying to find the answer for this important question (Fig. 1). May 13 : 11 cases, including 3 deaths. - A: April 1, Argentina:Departure with no reported symptomatic passengers. - B: At sea, April 6 :Index case symptoms, 70 year old Dutch man , April 11 died from respiratory distress. - C: St.Helena, April 24 :23 passengers disembark to return to their countries.The wife of the index case (now the 2nd case) disembark to Johannesburg. - D: Johannesburg, April 27, 2nd case died, 3rd case confirmed. - E: At sea, May 2, 4th case died on board. - F: At sea, May 4, two new cases suspected. - G: Switzerland, May 4, one confirmed case. - H: Netherlands, May 6, two confirmed cases. - I: France, May 10, one confirmed case. - J: United sataes, May 10, one confirmed case. History of hantaviruses During the Korean Peninsula war (from 1950 till1953), about three thousands of the soldier of the United Nations Command developed hemorrhagic fever and features of acute kidney injury, that was then described as hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS). The definite etiology of this disease was not discovered until 1981, when Lee et al. described HTNV, and its presence in the lungs of the striped field mouse (Apodemus agrarius). Nephropathia epidemica (NE), a condition resembling HFRS but less severe, was described in Fennoscandia since the early 1930s, but its causative virus, the PUUV was discovered in rodents bank voles in the year 1980 in Finland [2]. In 1993, an outbreak of a severe pulmonary condition occurred in the Four Corners region of the USA, with a rapidly progressive course resulting in adult respiratory distress syndrome.This was discovered to be a result of infection with a new hantavirus the SNV and the disease was termed hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS) [3]. This was the first description of this syndrome and the first identification of hantavirus diseases in the Americas. In subsequent years, additional hantaviruses responsible for HCPS were identified throughout the Americas, with ANDV being recognized in 1995. The first documented man-to-man transmission of ANDV occurred during an outbreak in El Bolsón, Argentina, in 1996. Since that event, several sporadic outbreaks involving interpersonal transmission have also been described [4]. Currently, more than 28 hantavirus species pathogenic to humans have been identified globally, causing a broad clinical spectrum ranging from acute kidney injury and pulmonary edema to severe hemorrhagic manifestations. Although approximately 1,000 HCPS cases have been documented, the annual global burden of HFRS is estimated to exceed 100,000 cases. Because hantavirus infections are frequently underdiagnosed and underreported in many regions, additional unidentified hantaviruses may still exist [3]. Morphological characteristics and genomic structure According to the international committee of taxonomy of viruses, the hantaviruses now belong to the family Hantaviridae of the Class Bunyaviricetes [2]. The virion of hantaviruses is spherical with a diameter of 120–160 nm (Fig. 2).The viral envelop is composed of a 5 nm thickness lipid bilayer containing embedded glycoprotein surface spikes extending to 10 nm from the lipid bilayer [5]. Similar to most of the members of the Class Bunyaviricetes, the hantavirus genome consists of three single-stranded, negative-sense RNA segments designated as large (L), medium (M), and small (S). The L segment encodes the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which is essential for viral RNA transcription and genome replication. The M segment produces a glycoprotein precursor that undergoes cleavage by host-cell signal peptidases to generate the surface glycoproteins Gn and Gc. These glycoproteins mediate attachment to host-cell receptors and play a major role in triggering immune activation and antibody production. The S segment encodes the nucleocapsid (N) protein, which play a role in stabilization of viral RNA. In some hantaviruses, including ANDV, PUUV, SNV, and Tula virus, the S genomic segment encodes a nonstructural protein capable of suppressing interferon production within host cells [7]. Cell entery and viral replication Hantavirus infection begins with the attachment of viral glycoprotein surface spikes to specific receptors on host cell membranes (Fig. 3). In certain hantaviruses, cell entry may also involve integrins, which are transmembrane heterodimeric glycoproteins. After receptor binding, the virus enters host cells through endocytic pathways or macropinocytosis, which may be clathrin-, caveolin-, or cholesterol-dependent. For example, HTNV and SEOV primarily utilize clathrin-mediated endocytosis, whereas ANDV can enter cells via cholesterol-dependent macropinocytosis [8]. After entering the host cell, virions become enclosed within clathrin-coated vesicles originating from specialized clathrin-coated areas of the plasma membrane [9]. The particles are then trafficked sequentially to early endosomes and subsequently to late endosomal and lysosomal compartments. Fusion of the viral envelope with the endosomal membrane enables the release of viral ribonucleoprotein complexes into the cytoplasm, where RdRp mediates viral genome replication and transcription. Messenger RNAs transcribed from the L and S segments are translated by free ribosomes in the cytoplasm, while those derived from the M segment are translated on membrane-bound ribosomes associated with the endoplasmic reticulum and rough endoplasmic reticulum. Newly synthesized virions are assembled within the Golgi apparatus and are thereafter transported to the cell surface for release via exocytosis [10]. Transmission, ecology and epidemiology Although hantaviruses are susceptible to inactivation by heating at 60 °C for 30 min, exposure to ultraviolet light, and treatment with organic or hypochlorite-based solvents [3], they remain relatively stable in environmental conditions. The viruses are capable of surviving for approximately 10 days at room temperature and for about 18 days at lower temperatures such as 4 °C [11], which contributes significantly to their potential transmission. In contrast to many other bunyaviruses, which are spread by insect vectors, hantaviruses infecting humans are primarily rodent-borne [12]. Most hantavirus infections in rodents are asymptomatic and persistent. An exception is infection of Syrian hamsters with ANDV, which can produce clinical manifestations resembling HCPS [13]. Several small mammals, including rodents, moles and shrews act as natural reservoirs for distinct hantavirus species and are capable of transmitting the virus through both horizontal and vertical routes (Fig. 4). Transmission among rodents occurs through droplet or aerosols generated from contaminated secretions and excreta such as urine, feces, and saliva, as well as contaminated food, biting, and scratching [13]. Fecal–oral transmission has also been documented, and infected females may pass the virus transplacentally to their offspring [6]. Behaviors such as mating and fighting further facilitate viral spread among susceptible animals. Humans typically acquire hantavirus infection through inhalation of aerosolized particles contaminated with rodent excreta or through ingestion of contaminated food [14]. Bites or scratches also represent possible routes of human transmission [6]. ANDV is currently the only hantavirus known to spread directly from person to person, usually after close contact with infected individuals .The virus may be present in saliva and transmitted through respiratory droplets generated by coughing or sneezing [15]. Evidence also suggests that ANDV can be transmitted via breast milk, as well as through placental and digestive routes [16]. Additional studies indicate that mites may contribute to the transmission of HFRS to both animals and humans [17]. Pigs have also been shown to acquire HTNV infection without developing severe disease [18]. Antibodies against HTNV and PUUV have additionally been identified in other animals like rabbits and cattle, while SNV antibodies have been detected in dogs and cats [19]. Nevertheless, the ecological significance of these animal hosts in hantavirus transmission remains unclear. The geographic patterns of human hantavirus infections closely correspond to the distribution of their rodent reservoir hosts. Consequently, HFRS is predominantly reported in Europe and Asia, caused by the so-called “old world hantaviruses”, whereas HCPS has been documented exclusively in the Americas, with specific hantavirus species, the so-called “new world hantaviruses” occurring in regions where their natural hosts are prevalent [20]. Currently, the annual global incidence of HFRS is estimated at approximately 100,000 cases. China remains the most highly endemic country, contributing nearly 70–90% of all reported HFRS cases, with about 40,000–60,000 cases recorded annually in recent years [21]. Nevertheless, the actual burden of infection is likely underestimated, as asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic cases probably exceed the number of clinically apparent infections. Furthermore, hantavirus infection is not classified as a notifiable disease in all countries where human cases have been identified [22]. Epidemiology of old world hantaviruses To date, seven hantavirus species have been linked to HFRS .In Asia, the most severe forms of the disease are mainly attributed to HTNV and Amur/Soochong virus infections, with case fatality of approximately 15%. By contrast, SEOV infection is generally associated with less severe clinical course and a lower case fatality rate of about 1–2%. Each year, an estimated 40,000–60,000 HFRS cases are documented, with nearly 99% reported from China [3]. Across Europe, more than 9,000 HFRS cases are reported annually, the majority resulting from PUUV infection. PUUV is widely distributed throughout northern and central Europe, the Balkan Peninsula, and parts of Russia, corresponding to the habitat of its rodent reservoir, Myodes glareolus. Infection with PUUV typically produces the milder form NE. The highest numbers of PUUV-related cases have been recorded in Finland, Sweden, and regions of European Russia. During recent years, reports of NE cases are increasing in Germany and Belgium, with additional cases reported in Norway, France, Austria, Hungary, Slovenia, and neighboring countries [23]. In Europe, severe HFRS cases are mainly caused by DOBV carried by Apodemus flavicollis. Although the geographic distribution of this rodent host extends beyond southeastern Europe, severe DOBV-associated infections have thus far been recognized primarily within the Balkan region [24]. Another hantavirus lineage, SAAV (DOBV-Aa), which is associated with Apodemus agrarius, has been identified as the cause of a comparatively mild form of HFRS in countries including Russia and Slovakia .Unlike DOBV-associated HFRS in the Balkans, where mortality rates may approach 12% ,fatal outcomes have not been reported in SAAV-related infections. More recently, an additional DOBV lineage was detected in Apodemus ponticus rodents from the Sochi region of European Russia and was linked to intermediate severity of HFRS manifestations. The variation in disease severity among the genetically related DOBV lineages may partly reflect differences in their respective rodent reservoir hosts [6]. Tula virus has also been implicated in human disease in Germany, Switzerland, and the Czech Republic; however, definitive evidence confirming its pathogenic role in humans remains limited [6]. Serological investigations have further identified evidence of human hantavirus exposure in countries such as Italy, Latvia, Lithuania, Moldova, Spain, and the United Kingdom .Nevertheless, confirmed clinical cases have not yet been formally documented in these regions [25]. Epidemiology of new world hantaviruses HCPS was first identified as a hantavirus-associated disease in 1993 in the Four Corners region of the United States. Since that initial outbreak, confirmed HCPS cases have also been documented in Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Panama, Paraguay, and Uruguay. At least 15 hantavirus species have been associated with HCPS in the Americas. Approximately 200 cases are reported annually across North and South America combined. Although the incidence of HCPS is considerably lower than that of HFRS, the syndrome is associated with a substantially higher mortality rate, averaging nearly 40%. SNV represents the principal etiologic agent of HCPS in the United States, whereas ANDV is the predominant causative virus in South America [20]. Importantly, ANDV remains the only hantavirus conclusively shown to undergo person-to-person transmission, and infections are associated with high fatality rates [26]. Similar to HFRS-associated hantaviruses, HCPS-related viruses may also produce asymptomatic or subclinical infections that remain unrecognized. Supporting this observation, several seroepidemiological studies have demonstrated relatively high antibody prevalence in certain populations despite the low number of clinically diagnosed HCPS cases [25]. Hantavirus infection in Africa The first serological indications of hantavirus infection in Africa were documented in 1984, when Gonzalez et al. detected hantavirus antibodies in rodents and humans from the Central African Republic, Gabon, Burkina Faso, and Benin [27]. Later investigations also provided serological evidence of human exposure in several other African countries, including Guinea, Senegal, Nigeria, Djibouti, and Egypt [25]. Nevertheless, despite these seroepidemiological findings, the literature has reported only a single suspected African case of HFRS to date [28] Pathogenesis of hantaviruses The severity of hantavirus infection in humans differs according to the viral species and strain involved [14]. HFRS predominantly involves the kidneys and vascular system, HCPS mainly targets the lungs and heart. Additional organs, including the liver, spleen, and nervous system, may also be affected. In both humans and animals, hantaviruses primarily infect the endothelial cells in the lung or the kidney as well as macrophages [29]. The principal pathological features of the described hantavirus diseases include increased vascular permeability and acute decrease of the platelet count [3]. Infection is initiated through binding of the viral glycoproteins Gn and Gc to β-integrin receptors, such as αvβ1 and αvβ3, located on the membranes of target cells [29]. Immature dendritic cells situated near endothelial cells transport virions from lymphatic vessels to regional lymph nodes, facilitating further infection of endothelial cells. These dendritic cells also function as antigen-presenting cells, triggering immune responses involving macrophages and CD8 + T lymphocytes [3]. A delayed type I interferon response contributes to elevated viral loads. Following immune activation, cytotoxic T cells release pro-inflammatory cytokines that damage infected endothelial cells, thereby increasing vascular permeability and promoting inflammation [29]. In addition, complement activation and secretion of inflammatory mediators—including interferons, interleukins such as IL-1, IL-6, and IL-10, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) have major roles in altering endothelial barrier function. The nature and strenghth of the immune response vary among different hantavirus species.Patients with HFRS commonly exhibit increased levels of CD8 + T lymphocytes. Elevated concentrations of IL-6 have been linked to thrombocytopenia and impaired renal function [30]. Human leukocyte antigen (HLA) molecules play an essential role in presenting vira…

full_text_sections.full_text: Abstract The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns regarding the pandemic potential of hantaviruses, particularly because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proved human-to-human transmission. These concerns and fears are justified by the profound negative impact of the recent COVID-19 pandemic. Hantaviruses are segmented negative-sense RNA viruses maintained mainly in rodents and transmitted to man primarily through inhalation of contaminated aerosols. Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome predominates in Europe and Asia, whereas hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome occurs in North and South Americas and carries high mortality. Although most hantaviruses have no human-to-human transmissibility, ANDV outbreaks demonstrate that transmission between humans is possible under close-contact conditions. Comparison with previous pandemics and WHO priority pathogens highlights several pandemic-associated features shared by hantaviruses, including zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences. In this review, we will try to find the answer for the question “Can hantaviruses cause the next pandemic?”. Similar content being viewed by others Introduction On May 2, 2026, a deadly outbreak of hantavirus infection on the cruise ship MV Hondius in the Atlantic Ocean was reported. At that time, according to the ship operator, 147 passengers and crew members remained onboard, while 34 individuals had previously disembarked. By 13 May 2026, the ongoing outbreak had resulted in a total of 11 confirmed cases and three deaths, corresponding to a case fatality rate of approximately 27%.The early reported cases were laboratory-confirmed as Andes virus (ANDV) infections [1]. ANDV is a member of the family Hantaviridae .Other members of the family are Hantaan virus (HTNV), Seoul virus (SEOV), Dobrava-Belgrade virus (DOBV), Puumala virus (PUUV) and Sin Nombre virus (SNV) and all are highly pathogenic to humans [2]. In this article, we will review the current knowledge on hantaviruses, and lessons from the past pandemics, trying to find the answer for this important question (Fig. 1). analysis.background: The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns about the pandemic potential of hantaviruses because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proven human‑to‑human transmission.
analysis.methods: This article is a narrative review; specific study design, sample collection, or analytical methods are not reported in the supplied evidence.
analysis.results: The outbreak resulted in 11 laboratory‑confirmed ANDV cases and three deaths (≈27% case‑fatality rate) and shows that human‑to‑human transmission can occur under close‑contact conditions; hantaviruses share pandemic‑associated features such as zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences.
analysis.contribution: The review synthesizes current knowledge to highlight characteristics that could allow hantaviruses to cause a pandemic, aiding surveillance and public‑health preparedness.
analysis.limitations: Only a single outbreak is described and no original data are presented; the available material is limited to an abstract and partial text.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus raised concerns about hantavirus pandemic potential because ANDV uniquely transmits between humans. Methods: This is a narrative review; specific methods are not reported. Results: The outbreak yielded 11 confirmed cases and three deaths (≈27% CFR) demonstrating possible human‑to‑human spread, and hantaviruses share pandemic‑linked traits like zoonotic origin and RNA variability. Contribution: The review highlights features that could enable hantaviruses to cause a pandemic, informing surveillance and preparedness. Limitations: Only one outbreak is examined, no original data are provided, and only abstract/partial text are available.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 会引起流行病吗?

analysis_zh.background: 2026 年 MV Hondius 爆发的 安第斯病毒 (ANDV) 再次引发了人们对 汉坦病毒es 的大流行潜力的担忧,因为 ANDV 是唯一已证明可人际传播的 汉坦病毒。
analysis_zh.methods: 本文是一篇叙述性评论;所提供的证据中未报告具体的研究设计、样本收集或分析方法。
analysis_zh.results: 此次疫情导致 11 例实验室确诊 ANDV 病例和 3 例死亡(约 27% 病死率),表明在密切接触条件下可能发生人际传播; 汉坦病毒 具有与大流行相关的特征,例如人畜共患病起源、RNA 基因组变异和环境影响。
analysis_zh.contribution: 该综述综合了当前的知识,强调了可能导致汉坦病毒大流行的特征,有助于监测和公共卫生准备。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅描述了一次疫情,没有提供原始数据;可用的材料仅限于摘要和部分文本。

papers.title: Hantavirus Diseases: A Review of Virology, Clinical Features, and Therapeutic Approaches
papers.abstract: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic infections that cause two main human diseases: Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS) in Eurasia and Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) in the Americas . These diseases are acquired mostly by inhaling aerosolized excreta from infected rodent reservoirs but transmission may also occur by direct contact or, rarely, person-to-person transmission with some South American strains. Hantaviruses produce severe morbidity and mortality, the severity of which depends on the viral species, the host immunological response, and the timeliness of medical assistance. Much has been learned over the last several decades from large-scale studies on the distribution of reservoir hosts, environmental determinants of virus persistence, and spillover into human populations that provide essential insight into hantavirus ecology. Significant progress has also been achieved in understanding molecular virology and immunopathogenesis, revealing that much of the clinical severity is due to immune-mediated vascular leakage rather than direct viral cytopathic effects. Yet despite the scientific improvements, there are still big gaps. There are no licensed vaccines for global use and antiviral treatments are limited in scope and efficacy with supportive care being the mainstay of clinical management . Diagnostic methods are improving, but species-specific identification is problematic in locations where numerous hantaviruses cocirculate. Furthermore, environmental change, urbanization, and changing rodent population dynamics are providing more opportunities for viral development and global spread. These diseases are acquired mostly by inhaling aerosolized excreta from infected rodent reservoirs but transmission may also occur by direct contact or, rarely, person-to-person transmission with some South American strains. Hantaviruses produce severe morbidity and mortality, the severity of which depends on the viral species, the host immunological response, and the timeliness of medical assistance. Much has been learned over the last several decades from large-scale studies on the distribution of reservoir hosts, environmental determinants of virus persistence, and spillover into human populations that provide essential insight into hantavirus ecology. Significant progress has also been achieved in understanding molecular virology and immunopathogenesis, revealing that much of the clinical severity is due to immune-mediated vascular leakage rather than direct viral cytopathic effects. Yet despite the scientific improvements, there are still big gaps. There are no licensed vaccines for global use and antiviral treatments are limited in scope and efficacy with supportive care being the mainstay of clinical management . analysis.background: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic agents causing Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome in Eurasia and Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome in the Americas, prompting a review of their impact.
analysis.main_directions: The review discusses transmission via aerosolized rodent excreta and occasional person‑to‑person spread, molecular virology and immune‑mediated pathogenesis, the lack of licensed vaccines and limited antivirals, diagnostic challenges in co‑circulating species, and the influence of environmental change on spillover.
analysis.current_state: Severity of disease varies with viral species, host immune response, and timely care, and current evidence attributes major clinical damage to immune‑driven vascular leakage rather than direct cytopathic effects; large‑scale ecological studies have clarified reservoir distribution and environmental determinants.
analysis.gaps: Key gaps include the absence of globally licensed vaccines, limited efficacy of antiviral therapies, difficulties in species‑specific diagnostics, and uncertainties about how urbanization and rodent dynamics will affect future spread.
analysis.future_research: Future work should focus on vaccine development, antiviral trials, improved species‑level diagnostics, longitudinal ecological surveillance, and mechanistic studies of immunopathogenesis to guide prevention and control.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic agents causing HFRS in Eurasia and HPS in the Americas, prompting a review of their impact. Main directions: The review discusses transmission via aerosolized rodent excreta and occasional person‑to‑person spread, molecular virology and immune‑mediated pathogenesis, the lack of licensed vaccines and limited antivirals, diagnostic challenges in co‑circulating species, and the influence of environmental change on spillover. Current state: Severity of disease varies with viral species, host immune response, and timely care, and current evidence attributes major clinical damage to immune‑driven vascular leakage rather than direct cytopathic effects; large‑scale ecological studies have clarified reservoir distribution and environmental determinants. Gaps: Key gaps include the absence of globally licensed vaccines, limited efficacy of antiviral therapies, difficulties in species‑specific diagnostics, and uncertainties about how urbanization and rodent dynamics will affect future spread. Future research: Future work should focus on vaccine development, antiviral trials, improved species‑level diagnostics, longitudinal ecological surveillance, and mechanistic studies of immunopathogenesis to guide prevention and control.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 疾病:病毒学、临床特征和治疗方法综述

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒 是世界范围内的人畜共患病原体,在欧亚大陆引起 肾综合征出血热,在美洲引起 汉坦病毒肺综合征,因此需要对其影响进行审查。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 该综述讨论了通过雾化啮齿动物排泄物传播和偶尔的人际传播、分子病毒学和免疫介导的发病机制、缺乏许可的疫苗和有限的抗病毒药物、共同循环物种的诊断挑战以及环境变化对溢出的影响。
analysis_zh.current_state: 疾病的严重程度因病毒种类、宿主免疫反应和及时护理而异,目前的证据将主要临床损害归因于免疫驱动的血管渗漏,而不是直接的细胞病变效应;大规模生态研究已经阐明了水库分布和环境决定因素。
analysis_zh.gaps: 主要差距包括缺乏全球许可的疫苗、抗病毒疗法的功效有限、物种特异性诊断的困难以及城市化和啮齿动物动态将如何影响未来传播的不确定性。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：汉坦病毒 是世界范围内的人畜共患病原体,在欧亚大陆引起 肾综合征出血热,在美洲引起 汉坦病毒肺综合征,因此需要对其影响进行审查。 主要方向：该综述讨论了通过雾化啮齿动物排泄物传播和偶尔的人际传播、分子病毒学和免疫介导的发病机制、缺乏许可的疫苗和有限的抗病毒药物、共同循环物种的诊断挑… 研究现状：疾病的严重程度因病毒种类、宿主免疫反应和及时护理而异,目前的证据将主要临床损害归因于免疫驱动的血管渗漏,而不是直接的细胞病变效应;大规模生态研… 不足：主要差距包括缺乏全球许可的疫苗、抗病毒疗法的功效有限、物种特异性诊断的困难以及城市化和啮齿动物动态将如何影响未来传播的不确定性。 后续研究：未报告

papers.title: Expedition cruises, island hopping, and zoonotic risk: governance and operational lessons from the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak
papers.abstract: The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a probable land-based zoonotic exposure can become a multinational public health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard environments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal. Although hantavirus infection is classically associated with exposure to infected rodents or contaminated environments, Andes virus is exceptional among orthohantaviruses because limited person-to-person transmission has been documented, particularly after close and prolonged contact. This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition-era travel medicine. Existing International Health Regulations, WHO ship-event guidance, and ECDC recommendations provide essential foundations for coordination, notification, isolation, and contact tracing; however, this outbreak exposed expedition-specific gaps in safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard recognition of nonspecific febrile illness, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post-disembarkation monitoring. We therefore propose an accountability-oriented One Health preparedness model that operationalizes existing guidance through route-level risk assessment, exposure-history assessment, onboard syndromic surveillance, isolation and telemedicine triggers, reference-laboratory pathways, port-of-call agreements, auditable passenger and excursion records, and cross-border post-travel monitoring. The central lesson is not that expedition cruises, birdwatching, or ecological tourism are inherently unsafe, but that their expanding geographic reach requires binding, auditable, and expedition-specific outbreak protocols before passengers embark.

Methods: The paper is a Perspective that analyses this outbreak as a case study of governance and operational gaps. papers.title_zh: 探险游轮、跳岛游和人畜共患病风险:MV Hondius Andes 汉坦病毒 疫情的治理和运营经验教训

analysis_zh.background: MV Hondius 探险游轮上的安第斯山脉 正汉坦病毒 疫情表明,当与封闭的船上环境、延迟的临床识别、远程导航、医疗疏散和国际乘客疏散相交叉时,可能的陆基人畜共患病暴露如何可能成为跨国公共卫生事件。
analysis_zh.methods: 本视角使用 MV Hondius 疫情作为分析案例研究,以确定探险时代旅行医学的治理和运营差距。
analysis_zh.results: 此次疫情暴露了探险队在安全港口准入、医疗后送、船上非特异性发热性疾病识别、诊断升级、乘客可追溯性和下船后监测等方面的具体差距。
analysis_zh.contribution: 现有的《国际卫生条例》、世卫组织船舶事件指南和 ECDC 建议为协调、通知、隔离和接触者追踪提供了重要基础;作者得出的结论是,探险游轮在乘客登船之前需要有具有约束力的、可审计的、专门针对探险的疫情协议。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅提供摘要;所提供的证据中未报告详细的方法、定量结果、样本量、位置、宿主信息和其他细节。

papers.summary_zh: 背景:MV Hondius 探险游轮上的安第斯山脉 正汉坦病毒 疫情表明,当与封闭的船上环境、延迟的... 方法:本视角使用 MV Hondius 疫情作为分析案例研究,以确定探险时代旅行医学的治理和运营差距。 结果:此次疫情暴露了探险队在安全港口准入、医疗后送、船上非特异性发热性疾病识别、诊断升级、乘客可追溯... 贡献:现有的《国际卫生条例》、世卫组织船舶事件指南和 ECDC 建议为协调、通知、隔离和接触者追踪提... 局限:仅提供摘要;所提供的证据中未报告详细的方法、定量结果、样本量、位置、宿主信息和其他细节。

papers.title: Ribavirin post-exposure prophylaxis for Andes virus exposure: a viewpoint.
papers.abstract: Andes virus (ANDV) is unique among hantaviruses because person-to-person transmission is possible. This raises questions on the relevance of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP), particularly following high-risk household, healthcare-associated, or laboratory exposures, considering its high case fatality rate. Ribavirin has demonstrated antiviral activity against hantaviruses in vitro and in animal models, although clinical evidence supporting its use for ANDV remains extremely limited. This viewpoint summarises the currently available evidence regarding ribavirin as PEP after potential ANDV exposure. We discuss the knowledge gaps that may limit the applicability of ribavirin PEP, to make informed decisions on the use of ribavirin in the setting of PEP. Potential dosing strategies are visualised by modelling and simulation, and potential dose and duration are discussed. Although the biological rationale for ribavirin PEP appears compelling, absence of controlled human studies and potential toxicity currently limit its role to highly selected exposure scenarios and use shortly after exposure.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) is unique among hantaviruses because it can spread person‑to‑person, and its high case‑fatality rate raises the question of post‑exposure prophylaxis (PEP).
analysis.main_directions: The viewpoint discusses the relevance of ribavirin PEP after high‑risk household, healthcare‑associated, or laboratory exposures and presents modelling of possible dosing strategies.
analysis.current_state: Ribavirin has demonstrated antiviral activity against hantaviruses in vitro and in animal models, but clinical evidence supporting its use for ANDV PEP remains extremely limited.
analysis.gaps: Key gaps include the absence of controlled human studies, uncertainty about optimal dose and duration, and concerns about ribavirin toxicity.
analysis.future_research: Future work should include controlled clinical trials, pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic studies, and safety monitoring to define ribavirin’s efficacy and safe regimens for ANDV PEP.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus can transmit person-to-person and has a high fatality rate, prompting interest in PEP. Main directions: The article reviews ribavirin PEP relevance after high‑risk exposures and models dosing strategies. Current state: Ribavirin shows in‑vitro and animal efficacy, but human clinical data for ANDV PEP are scarce. Gaps: No controlled human trials, unclear optimal dosing, and potential toxicity limit use. Future research: Conduct controlled trials, PK/PD studies, and safety monitoring to establish efficacy and safe regimens.

papers.title_zh: 利巴韦林暴露后预防[ PDI0008 ]: 观点

analysis_zh.background: 安第斯病毒 (ANDV) 在 汉坦病毒 中是独一无二的,因为它可以在人与人之间传播,并且其高病死率引发了暴露后预防 (PEP) 的问题。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 该观点讨论了高风险家庭、医疗保健相关或实验室暴露后利巴韦林 PEP 的相关性,并提出了可能的剂量策略的模型。
analysis_zh.current_state: 利巴韦林已在体外和动物模型中表现出针对 汉坦病毒es 的抗病毒活性,但支持其用于 ANDV PEP 的临床证据仍然极其有限。
analysis_zh.gaps: 主要差距包括缺乏对照人体研究、最佳剂量和持续时间的不确定性以及对利巴韦林毒性的担忧。
analysis_zh.future_research: 未来的工作应包括对照临床试验、药代动力学/药效学研究和安全性监测,以确定利巴韦林对 ANDV PEP 的疗效和安全方案。

papers.summary_zh: 背景:安第斯病毒 (ANDV) 在 汉坦病毒 中是独一无二的,因为它可以在人与人之间传播,并且其高病... 主要方向:该观点讨论了高风险家庭、医疗保健相关或实验室暴露后利巴韦林 PEP 的相关性,并提出了可能的剂... 研究现状:利巴韦林已在体外和动物模型中表现出针对 汉坦病毒es 的抗病毒活性,但支持其用于 ANDV P... 不足:主要差距包括缺乏对照人体研究、最佳剂量和持续时间的不确定性以及对利巴韦林毒性的担忧。 后续研究:未来的工作应包括对照临床试验、药代动力学/药效学研究和安全性监测,以确定利巴韦林对 ANDV...

papers.title: Modeling the Transmission Dynamics of Hantavirus with Environmental Viral Persistence
papers.abstract: Hantavirus is a zoonotic infection maintained principally in rodent reservoirs, and human exposure occurs mainly through environments contaminated with infectious viral particles. This study formulates and analyses a deterministic compartmental model for hantavirus transmission involving susceptible and infected rodents, susceptible, exposed, infectious and recovered humans, and an environmental viral reservoir. The model includes logistic rodent growth, direct rodent-to-rodent transmission, indirect environmental transmission, viral shedding from infected classes and natural viral clearance. Fundamental qualitative properties of the system are established, including existence, uniqueness, positivity and boundedness of solutions within a feasible region. The disease-free equilibrium is obtained, and the basic reproduction number is derived using the next-generation matrix method. Stability analysis shows that the disease-free equilibrium is locally and globally asymptotically stable when R0 < 1, whereas disease persistence is associated with R0 > 1 and the existence of a biologically feasible endemic equilibrium. Local stability conditions for the endemic equilibrium are characterised through the Jacobian matrix and the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. The analysis indicates that environmental viral persistence provides an indirect pathway that can sustain transmission between infected rodents, susceptible rodents and humans. These findings suggest that reducing rodent infection, limiting environmental contamination and increasing viral clearance may help move the system below the epidemic threshold. The model offers a mathematically tractable framework for interpreting hantavirus persistence and for guiding future extensions involving seasonality, spatial structure, stochasticity and data-driven parameterisation.

analysis.background: Hantavirus is a zoonotic infection maintained principally in rodent reservoirs, with human exposure occurring mainly through environments contaminated with infectious viral particles (A1).
analysis.methods: The authors formulate and analyse a deterministic compartmental model that includes susceptible and infected rodents, susceptible, exposed, infectious and recovered humans, and an environmental viral reservoir, incorporating logistic rodent growth, direct rodent‑to‑rodent transmission, indirect environmental transmission, viral shedding and natural viral clearance (A2; A3).
analysis.results: The disease‑free equilibrium and the basic reproduction number (R0) are derived (A5); stability analysis shows the disease‑free equilibrium is locally and globally asymptotically stable when R0 < 1 and an endemic equilibrium exists when R0 > 1 (A6); the analysis indicates that environmental viral persistence provides an indirect pathway sustaining transmission among rodents and humans (A8).
analysis.contribution: The model provides a mathematically tractable framework for interpreting hantavirus persistence and for guiding future extensions such as seasonality, spatial structure, stochasticity and data‑driven parameterisation (A10).
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is provided, so detailed parameter values, empirical validation, and real‑world data are not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus is a rodent‑borne zoonosis transmitted to humans via contaminated environments (A1). Methods: A deterministic compartmental model linking rodents, humans and an environmental viral reservoir was constructed, including logistic rodent growth, direct and indirect transmission, shedding and clearance (A2; A3). Results: The basic reproduction number (R0) and disease‑free equilibrium were derived; R0 < 1 yields a stable disease‑free state, while R0 > 1 permits an endemic equilibrium, and environmental persistence can sustain transmission (A5; A6; A8). Contribution: The framework offers a tractable tool for understanding hantavirus persistence and informs future model extensions (A10). Limitations: Only an abstract is available, lacking detailed data, validation, and parameter estimates.

papers.title_zh: 利用环境病毒持久性对 汉坦病毒 的传播动力学进行建模

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒 是一种人畜共患感染,主要存在于啮齿动物宿主中,人类主要通过被传染性病毒颗粒 (A1) 污染的环境进行暴露。
analysis_zh.methods: 作者制定并分析了一个确定性区室模型,其中包括易感和受感染的啮齿动物、易感、暴露、感染和康复的人类,以及环境病毒库,其中包括逻辑啮齿动物生长、直接啮齿动物间传播、间接环境传播、病毒脱落和自然病毒清除(A2;A3)。
analysis_zh.results: 推导出无病平衡和基本繁殖数(R0)(A5);稳定性分析表明,当 R0 < 1 时,无病平衡局部和全局渐近稳定;当 R0 > 1 时,存在地方性平衡 (A6);分析表明,环境病毒的持续存在提供了维持啮齿动物和人类之间传播的间接途径(A8)。
analysis_zh.contribution: 该模型提供了一个数学上易于处理的框架,用于解释 汉坦病毒 持久性并指导未来的扩展,例如季节性、空间结构、随机性和数据驱动的参数化 (A10)。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅提供摘要,因此所提供的证据中未报告详细的参数值、经验验证和真实世界数据。

papers.summary_zh: 背景:汉坦病毒 是一种人畜共患感染,主要存在于啮齿动物宿主中,人类主要通过被传染性病毒颗粒 (A1)... 方法:作者制定并分析了一个确定性区室模型,其中包括易感和受感染的啮齿动物、易感、暴露、感染和康复的人... 结果:推导出无病平衡和基本繁殖数(R0)(A5);稳定性分析表明,当 R0 < 1 时,无病平衡局部... 贡献:该模型提供了一个数学上易于处理的框架,用于解释 汉坦病毒 持久性并指导未来的扩展,例如季节性、... 局限:仅提供摘要,因此所提供的证据中未报告详细的参数值、经验验证和真实世界数据。

papers.title: Enfermedades transmitidas por vectores: chagas, dengue, malaria y hantavirus
papers.abstract: Rol estratégico de la Atención Primaria: el personal médico de familia es fundamental para el cribado inicial, el manejo ambulatorio de los casos leves y la prevención del colapso hospitalario. Alta sospecha clínica: el diagnóstico inicial se fundamenta en vincular un síndrome febril inespecífico con un nexo epidemiológico claro (viajes recientes, picaduras o exposición a roedores/brotes cerrados). Identificación de «banderas rojas»: es prioritario reconocer los signos de alarma (como sangrado de mucosas, dolor abdominal intenso, compromiso respiratorio o descompensación cardíaca) para garantizar una derivación hospitalaria inmediata y oportuna. Acción comunitaria: la educación para el control del vector y la notificación epidemiológica son pilares indelegables en el abordaje integral desde el centro de salud.

papers.title: Hantavirus and Middle East Public Health Challenges: A Subject Review
papers.abstract: Hantaviruses are emerging zoonotic viruses belonging to the family Hantaviridae, which are typically found in rodent reservoirs and transmitted to humans through contact with contaminated saliva, aerosolized particles or excreta. There are two major clinical syndromes associated with human infections: hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) — mostly reported from Europe and Asia, and hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS) — mostly reported from the Americas. The Middle East has a relatively low incidence of hantavirus disease, but recent epidemiological studies have shown that the virus is circulating in several countries, including Türkiye and Iran. The emergence of hantavirus infections in this area has become a growing concern as the population increases, climate variations occur, the rodents' ecology is changing, the agriculture spreads and the surveillance of this situation is improving. There is limited surveillance, underdiagnosis, and a large clinical overlap with other febrile illnesses all of which are important challenges in quantifying the regional burden of disease. This review aims at summarizing the state of knowledge about the epidemiology of the hantavirus in the Middle East, focusing on geographical distribution, environmental factors, rodent hosts, epidemiology, clinical features, diagnostic methods and public health preparedness. Current data suggest that while human infections are rare, ecological conditions which could sustain hantavirus are present in several Middle Eastern countries, and therefore increased surveillance and research into interdisciplinary and One Health initiatives are needed. Creating closer ties between the medical, veterinary and environmental communities is crucial for early outbreak detection and prevention in the future.
papers.journal: European Journal of Ecology, Biology and Agriculture

analysis.background: Hantaviruses are emerging zoonotic viruses in rodent reservoirs transmitted to humans via saliva, aerosols, or excreta (A1).
analysis.main_directions: The review outlines geographic distribution, environmental drivers, rodent hosts, epidemiology, clinical features, diagnostics, and public‑health preparedness in the Middle East (A6).
analysis.current_state: Recent studies show low but detectable hantavirus circulation in countries such as Türkiye and Iran (A3), and while human cases are rare, ecological conditions that could sustain transmission are present (A7).
analysis.gaps: Surveillance is limited, cases are underdiagnosed, and clinical presentation overlaps with other febrile illnesses, hindering accurate burden estimation (A5).
analysis.future_research: Enhanced surveillance, interdisciplinary One‑Health initiatives, and stronger collaboration among medical, veterinary, and environmental sectors are recommended (A7, A8).

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses are emerging zoonoses transmitted from rodents to humans (A1). Main directions: The review covers distribution, ecology, hosts, epidemiology, clinical aspects, diagnostics, and preparedness in the Middle East (A6). Current state: Low but confirmed circulation in Türkiye, Iran and others, with rare human cases yet suitable ecological conditions (A3, A7). Gaps: Limited surveillance, underdiagnosis, and symptom overlap impede burden assessment (A5). Future research: Expand surveillance, adopt One‑Health approaches, and foster medical‑veterinary‑environmental collaboration (A7, A8).

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 和中东公共卫生挑战:主题回顾

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒es 是啮齿动物宿主中新出现的人畜共患病毒,通过唾液、气溶胶或排泄物传播给人类 (A1)。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 该综述概述了中东地区的地理分布、环境驱动因素、啮齿动物宿主、流行病学、临床特征、诊断和公共卫生准备情况 (A6)。
analysis_zh.current_state: 最近的研究表明,土耳其和伊朗等国家的 汉坦病毒 循环量较低但可检测到 (A3),虽然人类病例很少见,但存在可以维持传播的生态条件 (A7)。
analysis_zh.gaps: 监测有限,病例诊断不足,临床表现与其他发热性疾病重叠,阻碍了准确的负担估计 (A5)。
analysis_zh.future_research: 建议加强监测、跨学科的同一个健康计划以及医疗、兽医和环境部门之间加强合作(A7、A8)。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：汉坦病毒es 是啮齿动物宿主中新出现的人畜共患病毒,通过唾液、气溶胶或排泄物传播给人类 (A1)。 主要方向：该综述概述了中东地区的地理分布、环境驱动因素、啮齿动物宿主、流行病学、临床特征、诊断和公共卫生准备情况 (A6)。 研究现状：最近的研究表明,土耳其和伊朗等国家的 汉坦病毒 循环量较低但可检测到 (A3),虽然人类病例很少见,但存在可以维持传播的生态条件 (A7)。 不足：监测有限,病例诊断不足,临床表现与其他发热性疾病重叠,阻碍了准确的负担估计 (A5)。 后续研究：建议加强监测、跨学科的同一个健康计划以及医疗、兽医和环境部门之间加强合作(A7、A8)。

papers.title: Ecological Cascades and Future Hantavirus Spillover Risk in a Changing Climate
papers.abstract: Abstract Anthropogenic climate change is reshuffling global biodiversity and accelerating zoonotic spillover at the human-wildlife interface. The 2026 multi-country cruise ship outbreak of Andes virus highlights the unpredictable and globalized threat posed by rodent-borne orthohantaviruses, which cause severe, highly fatal human diseases worldwide. Despite this significant public health risk, macroecological synthesis linking environmental drivers to systemic shifts in hantavirus hazards has been lacking. Here, we implement an integrated ecological and machine learning framework combining 89 unique virus-host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic Force-of-Infection (FOI) modeling to project global spillover hazards under current and future climate scenarios (SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5) for 2041–2060. Our models demonstrate a spatially uneven but geographically extensive expansion of spillover hazards, with approximately 74.9% of modeled land area experiencing an increase in FOI. We identify three distinct high-hazard belts heavily concentrated in already-temperate reservoir ranges: western and central North America, central and northeastern Europe extending into western Russia, and discrete patches within central South America. Crucially, our findings reveal that hantavirus hazards are fundamentally shaped along virus-specific ecological axes. While thermal and precipitation anomalies predominate the transmission dynamics of Old and New World lineages like Sin-Nombre virus and Thailand virus, distinct urban-centric Profiles emerge for Seoul virus and Mamore virus, driven explicitly by fine-scale anthropogenic landscape modifications. These results reveal that climate change will not simply intensify viral spillover uniformly but will fundamentally restructure it across varied climatic and human-dominated environments. Our maps provide a critical baseline for targeted global surveillance and highlight the urgent need to integrate spatial conservation planning with public health preparedness.

Despite this significant public health risk, macroecological synthesis linking environmental drivers to systemic shifts in hantavirus hazards has been lacking. Here, we implement an integrated ecological and machine learning framework combining 89 unique virus-host associations across 61 reservoir species with mechanistic Force-of-Infection (FOI) modeling to project global spillover hazards under current and future climate scenarios (SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5) for 2041-2060. Our models demonstrate a spatially uneven but geographically extensive expansion of spillover hazards, with approximately 74.9% of modeled land area experiencing an increase in FOI. We identify three distinct high-hazard belts heavily concentrated in already-temperate reservoir ranges: western and central North America, central and northeastern Europe extending into western Russia, and discrete patches within central South America. Crucially, our findings reveal that hantavirus hazards are fundamentally shaped along virus-specific ecological axes. While thermal and precipitation anomalies predominate the transmission dynamics of Old and New World lineages like Sin-Nombre virus and Thailand virus, distinct urban-centric Profiles emerge for Seoul virus and Mamore virus, driven explicitly by fine-scale anthropogenic landscape modifications. These results reveal that climate change will not simply intensify viral spillover uniformly but will fundamentally restructure it across varied climatic and human-dominated environments. Our maps provide a critical baseline for targeted global surveillance and highlight the urgent need to integrate spatial conservation planning with public health preparedness.
